meta.title: Stroke Risk Calculator (CHA₂DS₂-VASc) — Atrial Fibrillation
meta.description: Estimate stroke risk in atrial fibrillation with the CHA₂DS₂-VASc score (ESC guideline). Annual risk percentage and anticoagulation guidance. Free, instant, no sign-up.

strokeRisk.title: Stroke Risk Calculator (CHA₂DS₂-VASc)
strokeRisk.description: Estimate your annual stroke risk in atrial fibrillation using the CHA₂DS₂-VASc score (Lip et al. 2010, ESC guideline 2020).
strokeRisk.instructions: Enter your age and sex, then check every risk factor that applies. The CHA₂DS₂-VASc score ranges from 0 to 9 — higher scores mean higher annual stroke risk.
strokeRisk.factor_heartFailure_desc: Clinical heart failure or left-ventricular ejection fraction ≤ 40 %
strokeRisk.factor_hypertension_desc: Treated hypertension or resting blood pressure > 140/90 mmHg
strokeRisk.factor_diabetes_desc: Fasting glucose > 125 mg/dL or on antidiabetic therapy
strokeRisk.factor_strokeHistory_desc: Previous ischemic stroke, transient ischemic attack, or systemic thromboembolism
strokeRisk.factor_vascularDisease_desc: CAD with prior MI, peripheral artery disease, or aortic plaque
strokeRisk.anticoagulation_consider: Anticoagulation may be considered — individual benefit-risk discussion
strokeRisk.anticoagulation_recommended: Oral anticoagulation recommended (DOAC preferred over warfarin)
strokeRisk.refTitle: Annual stroke risk by CHA₂DS₂-VASc score
strokeRisk.howItWorksText: The CHA₂DS₂-VASc score (Lip et al. 2010) is the international standard for stroke risk stratification in non-valvular atrial fibrillation. It assigns points for congestive heart failure (1), hypertension (1), age ≥ 75 (2), diabetes (1), prior stroke or TIA (2), vascular disease (1), age 65–74 (1) and female sex (1). Annual stroke rates were derived from a Swedish AF cohort (Friberg et al., Eur Heart J 2012, n = 90,490). The 2020 ESC guideline recommends oral anticoagulation from a score of 2 in men and 3 in women.
strokeRisk.disclaimer: This calculator is for informational purposes only and does not replace medical advice. The decision to anticoagulate must always be made by a clinician weighing the bleeding risk (HAS-BLED score). The score applies only to non-valvular atrial fibrillation.

faq.a: The CHA₂DS₂-VASc score (Lip et al. 2010) is a clinical scoring system that estimates annual stroke risk in patients with atrial fibrillation. It ranges from 0 to 9 points, derived from eight clinical risk factors including age, sex, and cardiovascular comorbidities.

Per the 2020 ESC guideline: in men from a score of 2, in women from a score of 3. With a score of 1 (men) or 2 (women) anticoagulation may be considered after individual benefit-risk assessment including bleeding risk.

faq.q: Why do women get an extra point?
faq.a: Female sex is associated with a slightly higher stroke risk in atrial fibrillation. However, female sex alone (score = 1 from sex only) is not an indication for anticoagulation — at least one additional risk factor must be present.

Direct oral anticoagulants (DOACs / NOACs) such as apixaban, rivaroxaban, edoxaban or dabigatran are first-line per the 2020 ESC guideline — except for mechanical heart valves or moderate-to-severe mitral stenosis, where warfarin (VKA) remains required.

faq.q: How high is stroke risk without treatment?
faq.a: In atrial fibrillation with a CHA₂DS₂-VASc score of 4 the annual stroke risk is around 4.8 %, at a score of 7 it rises to roughly 11 %. Adequate anticoagulation reduces this risk by about 64 %.

faq.q: Should bleeding risk also be assessed?
faq.a: Yes. Before any anticoagulation the HAS-BLED score should also be calculated to estimate bleeding risk. A high HAS-BLED is not a contraindication but a flag to address modifiable risk factors (blood pressure, alcohol, concomitant medication).
